## Breakdown

## Mixed-Effects Logistic Regression

To investigate how similarity difference ($simDiff$) interacts with both Task Type ($task\_type$) and Condition ($condition$) in predicting whether the focal character is chosen, we fit a generalized linear mixed model (GLMM) with a random intercept for character:

$$
\text{chosen} \sim \text{simDiff} \times \text{task\_type} \times \text{condition} + (1 \mid \text{character})
$$

In this specification, $simDiff$ reflects the surplus number of demographic features shared between participant and focal character, $task\_type$ distinguishes between Pairwise Choice vs. Full Ranking selection tasks, and $condition$ captures the framing as Casual or Competitive. The random intercept $(1 \mid \text{character})$ accounts for potential differences in baseline preferability across characters.

Model fit indicated that overall similarity ($simDiff$) strongly increases the log-odds of choosing the focal character. In our baseline scenario—Pairwise Choice under a Casual framing—each additional shared feature increased the odds by a multiplicative factor of approximately $\exp(0.325) \approx 1.38$. However, the slope of $simDiff$ was attenuated in each of the Full Ranking and Competitive conditions; specifically, the relevant two-way interactions ($simDiff:task\_type_{full\_ranking}$ and $simDiff:condition_{Competitive}$) were significantly negative. The three-way interaction ($simDiff:task\_type_{full\_ranking}:condition_{Competitive}$) was significantly positive, partially offsetting those reductions when both Full Ranking and Competitive were present simultaneously.

These results suggest that while surplus similarity is a strong predictor of focal character choice in the baseline context, its influence weakens when participants must provide full rankings rather than pairwise choices, or when the situation is framed competitively rather than casually. Moreover, certain characters were chosen far more frequently than others, indicated by a substantial random intercept variance ($\sigma^2 \approx 1.035$). This finding underscores the importance of adjusting for character-level effects when modeling the probability of being chosen.

Overall, this model confirms a robust effect of demographic similarity on choice behavior, modified significantly by both task format and contextual framing. In subsequent analyses, we explore whether individuals differ in the strength of their similarity-based preferences by introducing a random slope on $simDiff$ at the participant level.
